DISCOVERY 302, DEFENDANT HAR-BRO OF NORTHERN CALIFORNIA'S MOTION TO COMPEL PLAINTIFF TO PROVIDE FURTHER ANSWERS TO HER DEPOSITION; DEFENDANT'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER DEPOSITION TESTIMONY FROM THE PLAINTIFF IS GRANTED. PLAINTIFF HAS BROUGHT SUIT FOR, AMONG OTHER CLAIMS, FRAUD. PLAINTIFF TESTIFIED THAT A REPRESENTATIVE OF DEFENDANT HAD MADE UNTRUE STATEMENTS. HOWEVER, PLAINTIFF'S COUNSEL WOULD NOT PERMIT QUESTIONS DESIGNED TO IDENTIFY THOSE STATEMENTS AND THE BASIS FOR PLAINTIFF'S TESTIMONY THAT THE STATEMENTS WERE NOT TRUE. THESE OBJECTIONS WERE IMPROPER. CONTRARY TO PLAINTIFF'S ARGUMENT, DEFENDANT'S DEPOSITION QUESTIONS WERE NOT IMPERMISSIBLE CONTENTION INTERROGATORIES WITHIN THE MEANING OF RIFKIND V. SUPERIOR COURT, 22 CAL. APP. 4TH 1255 (1994). IN RIFKIND, THE DEPOSING ATTORNEY SOUGHT THE APPLICATION OF FACTS, AND A LISTING OF ALL THEM, TO THE LEGAL CONTENTION OF AN AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE. BY CONTRAST, HERE, DEFENDANT'S COUNSEL SOUGHT ONLY FACTUAL TESTIMONY REGARDING FACTS ABOUT WHICH PLAINTIFF TESTIFIED SHE KNEW. NOR ARE DEFENDANT'S QUESTION "UNFAIR" AS PLAINTIFF CONTENDS. THE QUESTIONS DO NOT "CALL UPON THE DEPONENT TO SORT OUT THE FACTUAL MATERIAL IN THE CASE ACCORDING TO SPECIFIC LEGAL CONTENTIONS." RATHER, THE QUESTION QUITE PROPERLY ASK THE PLAINTIFF FOR THE FACTUAL BASIS OF HER ASSERTIONS THAT CERTAIN STATEMENTS WERE UNTRUE WHEN MADE TO HER, BY ASKING HER TO IDENTIFY THOSE STATEMENTS AND ANY BASIS FOR CLAIMING THEY ARE UNTRUE. THIS LINE OF QUESTIONS IS PROPER. ACCORDINGLY, DEFENDANT'S MOTION IS GRANTED AND PLAINTIFF IS REQUIRED TO APPEAR FOR FURTHER DEPOSITION WITHIN 10 BUSINESS DAYS OF THIS ORDER. ORDER SIGNED. PRO TEM JUDGE: GEOFF HOWARD, CLERK: JACQUELINE ALAMEDA, NOT REPORTED. =(302/JPT)

LAW AND MOTION, 302, RULING: THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING WHICH WAS NOT CONTESTED. DEFENDANT HAR-BRO OF NORTHERN CALIFORNIA'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT IS OVERRULED IN ITS ENTIRETY. THE COMPLAINT PLEADS FRAUD SUFFICIENTLY. FURTHERMORE, WHILE THE COMPLAINT DOES NOT STATE THE TERMS OF THE CONTRACT VERBATIM, PLAINTIFF HAS PLED THE CONTRACT'S LEGAL INTENDMENT AND EFFECT. A CONTRACT SHOULD BE PLEADED EITHER IN HAEC VERBA OR ACCORDING TO ITS LEGAL INTENDMENT AND EFFECT. SCOLINOS V. KOLTS (1995), 37 CAL. APP. 4TH 635, 640. TO PLEAD A CONTRACT ACCORDING TO ITS LEGAL EFFECT, PLAINTIFF SHOULD ALLEGE THE MAKING AND THEN PROCEED TO ALLEGE THE SUBSTANCE OF ITS RELEVANT TERMS. SEE SNYDER V. UNITED PROPERTIES CO. (1921), 53 CAL.APP. 428, 431; PNEUCRETE CORP. V. UNITED STATES FIDELITY & GUARANTY CO. (1935), 7 CAL.APP.2D 733, 742. PLANTIFF HAS ALLEGED THE SUBSTANCE OF THE RELEVANT TERMS OF THE CONTRACT. FAC 139. FURTHERMORE, IT IS GENERALLY A QUESTION OF FACT WHETHER A PARTICULAR THIRD PERSON IS AN INTENDED BENEFICIARY OF A CONTRACT. PROUTY V. GORES TECHNOLOGY GROUP (2004), 121 CAL. APP. 4TH 1225, 1233. DEMURRER IS NOT THE PROPER STAGE TO INTERPRET THE CONTRACT AS A WHOLE WITHOUT EVIDENCE OF THE CIRCUMSTANCES AND NEGOTIATIONS OF THE PARTIES IN MAKING THE CONTRACT. (PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE A FORM OF ORDER.) JUDGE: RICHARD KRAMER; CLERK: GINA GONZALES; COURT REPORTER: ANTHONY C. VAUGHN, CSR # 6185

SEP. STATEMENT IN OPPOS. TO MTN TO COMPEL PLTF TO PROVIDE FURTHER ANSWERS TO HER DEPOSITION FILED BY PLAINTIFF CONNELL, DIANE

LAW AND MOTION, 302, RULING - A R G U E D; THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING. DEFENDANT FERMA CORPORATION'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION - THE MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT AND ALTERNATE MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION ARE EACH DENIED. DEFENDANT FERMA CORPORATION FAILED TO SHIFT THE BURDEN OF PRODUCTION OF EVIDENCE ON ANY CAUSE OF ACTION. REGARDING THE SIXTEENTH CAUSE OF ACTION, FERMA FAILED TO PRODUCE SUFFICIENT EVIDENCE TO SHOW THERE IS NO ISSUE OF MATERIAL FACT THAT IT DID NOT BREACH THE SUBCONTRACT FOR TWO REASONS. FIRST, PLAINTIFF DID NOT ADDRESS EACH BREACH ALLEGED BY PLAINTIFF IN THE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT. SECOND, FERMA ARGUES THAT IT DID NOT BREACH THE SUBCONTRACT BECAUSE IT HAD NO OBLIGATION TO GET A PERMIT (SSUFS 9, 11) OR TARP THE BUILDING DURING THE DEMOLITION PROCESS (SSUF 12). THESE ARGUMENTS DO NOT OBVIATE FERMA'S OBLIGATIONS AS SET FORTH IN THE SUBCONTRACT. (SEE SUBCONTRACT, TERMS AND CONDITIONS, 1, 5, AND 8.) THERE IS THUS AN ISSUE OF MATERIAL FACT AS TO FERMA'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER THE SUBCONTRACT. REGARDING THE THIRD, FOURTH, FIFTH, SIXTH AND SEVENTH CAUSES OF ACTION, THESE CAUSES OF ACTION AS PLED DO NOT ARISE SIMPLY FROM THE BREACH OF CONTRACT AND INVOLVE DUTIES "INDEPENDENT OF THE CONTRACT ARISING FROM PRINCIPLES OF TORT LAW." (ERLICH V. MENEZES (1999) 21 CAL.4TH 543, 551.) REGARDING THE EIGHTH CAUSE OF ACTION, FERMA FAILED TO SHOW THE ABSENCE OF AN ISSUE OF MATERIAL FACT AS TO WHETHER IT VIOLATED ANY STATUTE OR THAT THE SUBJECT CODE VIOLATIONS ARE NOT DESIGNED TO PROTECT THE PLAINTIFF. JUDGE: RICHARD KRAMER, CLERK: CYNTHIA HERBERT; COURT REPORTER: ANTHONY VAUGHN, CSR # 6185

LAW AND MOTION, 302, NO APPEARANCE ON THIS MATTER. THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING. DEFENDANT HAR-BRO OF NORTHERN CALIFORNIA'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT IS OVERRULED. THE COURT FINDS THAT CAL. PENAL CODE 496(C) PERMITS THEFT AND RECEIPT OF STOLEN PROPERTY TO BE PLED IN THE ALTERNATIVE. PLAINTIFF HAS PLED FACTS SUFFICIENT TO ESTABLISH THE THREE ELEMENTS NECESSARY FOR A CAUSE OF ACTION FOR RECEIPT OF STOLEN PROPERTY: (1) THE PROPERTY WAS STOLEN; (2) THE DEFENDANT KNEW THAT THE PROPERTY WAS STOLEN; AND (3) THE DEFENDANT HAD POSSESSION OF SUCH PROPERTY. (CASTILLO-CRUZ V. HOLDER (9TH CIR. 2009) 581 F.3D 1154, 1161.) (SEE COMPLAINT, 131.) (PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE A FORM OF ORDER.) JUDGE: LESLIE C. MANDATORY SETTLEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-27-2013 Off Calendar

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-26-2013 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling.
DEFENDANT HAR-BRO OF NORTHERN CALIFORNIA'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT is overruled. The Court finds that Cal. Penal Code 496(c) permits theft and receipt of stolen property to be pled in the alternative. Plaintiff has pled facts sufficient to establish the three elements necessary for a cause of action for receipt of stolen property: (1) the property was stolen; (2) the defendant knew that the property was stolen; and (3) the defendant had possession of such property. (Castillo-Cruz v. Holder (9th Cir. 2009) 581 F.3d 1154, 1161.) (See Complaint, 131.) (Prevailing party to prepare a form of order.) Judge: Leslie C. Nichols, Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Court Reporter: Anthony C. Vaughn, CSR # 6185 =(302/LCN)

MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Jul-26-2013 Continued To Jan-06-14 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Compel Pltf To Provide Further Answers To Her Deposition
Off Calendar Jul-12-2013 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Tony Stavjanik, Clapp Moroney, 925.734.0990, appearing for the Defendant Har-Bro;
No appearance by any other party or counsel.

The Court adopted the tentative ruling DEFENDANT HAR-BRO OF NORTHERN CALIFORNIA'S MOTION To Compel Plaintiff To Provide Further Answers To Her Deposition; Defendant's motion to compel further deposition testimony from the plaintiff is granted. Plaintiff has brought suit for, among other claims, fraud. Plaintiff testified that a representative of Defendant had made untrue statements. However, plaintiff's counsel would not permit questions designed to identify those statements and the basis for plaintiff's testimony that the statements were not true. These objections were improper. Contrary to plaintiff's argument, defendant's deposition questions were not impermissible contention interrogatories within the meaning of Rifkind v. Superior Court, 22 Cal. App. 4th 1255 (1994). In Rifkind, the deposing attorney sought the application of facts, and a listing of all them, to the legal contention of an affirmative defense. By contrast, here, defendant's counsel sought only factual testimony regarding facts about which plaintiff testified she knew. Nor are defendant's question "unfair" as plaintiff contends. The questions do not "call upon the deponent to sort out the factual material in the case according to specific legal contentions." Rather, the question quite properly ask the plaintiff for the factual basis of her assertions that certain statements were untrue when made to her, by asking her to identify those statements and any basis for claiming they are untrue. This line of questions is proper. Accordingly, defendant's motion is granted and plaintiff is required to appear for further deposition within 10 business days of this Order. Order signed. Pro Tem Judge: Geoff Howard, Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda, Not reported. =(302/JPT)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Jul-02-2013 Denied
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES - ATTY. JOHN H. PATTON OF PATTON & SULLIVAN LLP, TEL. NO. (925) 600-1800 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. JONATHAN THOMAS OF ARCHER & NORRIS, TEL. NO. (925) 930-6600 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT FERMA CORPORATION'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION - The motion for summary judgment and alternate motion for summary adjudication are each denied. Defendant Ferma Corporation failed to shift the burden of production of evidence on any cause of action. Regarding the sixteenth cause of action, Ferma failed to produce sufficient evidence to show there is no issue of material fact that it did not breach the Subcontract for two reasons. First, Plaintiff did not address each breach alleged by Plaintiff in the First Amended Complaint. Second, Ferma argues that it did not breach the Subcontract because it had no obligation to get a permit (SSUFs 9, 11) or tarp the building during the demolition process (SSUF 12). These arguments do not obviate Ferma's obligations as set forth in the Subcontract. (See Subcontract, Terms and Conditions, 1, 5, and 8.) There is thus an issue of material fact as to Ferma's obligations under the Subcontract. Regarding the third, fourth, fifth, sixth and seventh causes of action, these causes of action as pled do not arise simply from the breach of contract and involve duties "independent of the contract arising from principles of tort law." (Erlich v. Menezes (1999) 21 Cal.4th 543, 551.) Regarding the eighth cause of action, Ferma failed to show the absence of an issue of material fact as to whether it violated any statute or that the subject code violations are not designed to protect the Plaintiff. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Richard Kramer, Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR # 6185 =(302/RAK)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar May-22-2013 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance on this matter.

RULING: The Court adopted its tentative ruling which was not contested. DEFENDANT HAR-BRO OF NORTHERN CALIFORNIA's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT is overruled in its entirety. The complaint pleads fraud sufficiently. Furthermore, while the complaint does not state the terms of the contract verbatim, Plaintiff has pled the contract's legal intendment and effect. A contract should be pleaded either in haec verba or according to its legal intendment and effect. Scolinos v. Kolts (1995), 37 Cal. App. 4th 635, 640. To plead a contract according to its legal effect, Plaintiff should allege the making and then proceed to allege the substance of its relevant terms. See Snyder v. United Properties Co. (1921), 53 Cal.App. 428, 431; Pneucrete Corp. v. United States Fidelity & Guaranty Co. (1935), 7 Cal.App.2d 733, 742. Plantiff has alleged the substance of the relevant terms of the contract. FAC 139. Furthermore, it is generally a question of fact whether a particular third person is an intended beneficiary of a contract. Prouty v. Gores Technology Group (2004), 121 Cal. App. 4th 1225, 1233. Demurrer is not the proper stage to interpret the contract as a whole without evidence of the circumstances and negotiations of the parties in making the contract. (Prevailing party to prepare a form of order.) Judge: Richard Kramer; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Anthony C. Vaughn, CSR # 6185
